narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in key academic areas that the admissions model weighs heavily. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 28 is notably lower than the usual 36.70, and while you're planning full-time studies like most applicants, your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 1—far below the averages around 5.5—painted a picture of early underperformance. The model predicts a 44.2% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed professionally. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly signaled to the model that your academic trajectory in later undergrad years raised risks for bar success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest influence, pulling the prediction back toward passing by showing some resilience despite being below average. Your LSAT score followed as the third key driver, also nudging things positively even though it was low, as it still demonstrated baseline potential compared to the dataset. Overall, these top three elements—your Year 3 performance dragging the odds down the most, offset somewhat by GPA and LSAT—tipped the scales against you when stacked against typical students who boast higher deciles, GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 37. While full-time status aligns with the vast majority, it didn't sway the outcome here. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a white male from a middle-income home—many in similar situations rebound strongly. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your academic record through post-bac courses or a master's to lift those decile perceptions, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your growth. Reapply after these improvements, targeting schools with holistic reviews, and consider bar prep resources early to build confidence. You've got the drive; channeling it into these targeted steps can rewrite your story for the next cycle.
